title: list_rooms performs sequential N+1 DB writes inside a per-row reap loop
evidence: For each of N waiting rooms, the handler issues either a DELETE or an UPDATE inside a `for` loop (`await supabase.from('rooms').delete()…` / `await supabase.from('rooms').update(…)`). Each awaited call is a separate round-trip to Postgres. In a lobby with 20 waiting rooms where 10 need reaping, this function makes 1 SELECT + up to 10 individual DELETE/UPDATE calls before returning — O(N) sequential async DB round-trips.
impact: Each `list_rooms` call (polled by the lobby at a regular interval) can take O(N) × (DB round-trip latency) ms. On a t3.micro with even modest concurrency (e.g., 5 users polling at once) this serialises 50+ sequential Postgres round-trips per second, holding Supabase connection slots and adding visible latency to the lobby refresh.
recommendation: Batch the reap mutations in a single SQL call. Either (a) issue one `DELETE … WHERE id = ANY(ARRAY[…])` for fully-dead rooms and one `UPDATE rooms SET players = CASE id WHEN … END WHERE id = ANY(…)` for trimmed rooms after the loop, or (b) add a `reap_stale_rooms()` plpgsql function (analagous to `submit_room_action`) that does the full GC in one round-trip. Filtering with `.gte('created_at', …)` would also eliminate most candidates before the loop.
acceptance_criteria: list_rooms makes at most 2 DB calls (1 SELECT + 1 batched write) regardless of N
Reap logic tested with a batch of 20 stale rooms

title: AI forward-sim does up to ~3400 simulateImpact passes per turn on hard difficulty
evidence: `searchShot` sweeps `angle` in [5..175] step 1° (171 values) × `power` in [20..100] step 1 (81 values) on hard difficulty = 13,851 combinations. Each calls `simulateImpact` which runs a `stepProjectile`/`sweepCollide` loop up to SIM_MAX_TICKS=1600 per probe. A mid-range shot resolves in ~200 ticks, so a hard turn can cost 13,851 × ~200 = ~2.8 M `sweepCollide` calls. On easy the grid is 28×20=560 combinations. `sweepCollide` itself performs a sweep through the 720k-byte bitmap per step. This runs synchronously on the main thread in hot-seat.
impact: On a low-end mobile device, a hard-difficulty AI turn (maybeDriveAi is called every frame during PLAYER_TURN) could block the main thread for tens of milliseconds, causing a visible frame drop. The `computeAiPlan` call is inside a setTimeout callback in hot-seat (AI_AIM_DELAY 600ms), so it fires once per turn — not every frame — but its synchronous duration is still the concern.
recommendation: (a) Run the AI search in a Web Worker so the main thread stays unblocked. (b) Reduce hard tuning from angleStep=1/powerStep=1 to 2/2 (cutting candidates by 4×) and add a local `bestScore` early-exit if the best candidate within 5px is found before the full sweep. (c) Adaptive early-exit: if `score < (some near-hit threshold)` break inner loop. (d) Only compute once per turn (already done via `aiActedKey` — the 600ms delay ensures one call).
acceptance_criteria: AI planning does not block the main thread for more than 4ms on a mid-range mobile device (Chrome 120, Moto G5)
Behavior difference from reduced search is within ±2° aim accuracy at hard difficulty

title: drawExplosions creates a new RadialGradient object every frame per live burst
evidence: Inside `drawExplosions()`, for each live `Burst`, the code calls `ctx.createRadialGradient(b.cx, b.cy, 0, b.cx, b.cy, r)` (line 735) and adds 3 color stops every frame. A MIRV/cluster can produce 7–9 simultaneous bursts, each re-animated for ~30 frames (durationFrames), meaning up to 7×30 = 210 gradient allocations over the burst lifetime. `createRadialGradient` allocates a CanvasGradient object on the GPU-backed canvas context; creating and immediately discarding it every frame is GC pressure and prevents the browser from caching the gradient texture.
impact: At 60fps with 7 simultaneous bursts, this generates up to 420 CanvasGradient objects per second. While not catastrophic on desktop, on a t3.micro-served mobile game this contributes to GC pauses and jank. Medium severity because bursts are transient (not constant), and the object is small.
recommendation: Cache the CanvasGradient per burst by pre-building it at a normalized radius=1 and scaling with `ctx.setTransform`, OR re-create it only when `r` changes significantly (per-frame `r` changes because `grow` ramps over 18% then locks). Since the radius is fixed after the grow phase, the gradient can be cached from the `Burst` struct after the grow phase ends (`grow === 1` once `t >= GROW`). Alternatively, clamp creation to once per distinct integer-pixel radius bucket.
acceptance_criteria: Number of CanvasGradient allocations per burst lifetime is reduced from durationFrames to ≤2 (grow + settle phases)
Visual appearance is unchanged (same colors / transparency curve)

title: drawScorches creates a new RadialGradient per scorch decal per frame
evidence: Inside `drawScorches()`, every live `Scorch` calls `ctx.createRadialGradient(…)` each frame (line 825). Scorch lifetime is `ex.durationFrames * 3` (~90 frames for a standard blast). A cluster/MIRV spawns 7 scorches simultaneously, so 7×90 = 630 gradient allocations are made over a typical multi-bomblet resolution. Like `drawExplosions`, the gradient parameters change only via `alpha` (the color stops include it), which does change each frame — but the geometry (cx, cy, innerR, outerR) is constant over the scorch's life.
impact: Lower than performance-003 because scorches are longer-lived (more frames, fewer simultaneous) but less visually impactful. GC pressure and wasted GPU texture allocations. Lower magnitude than performance-003.
recommendation: Store the fixed-geometry gradient as a cached `CanvasGradient | null` field on each `Scorch` struct (or in a Map keyed by scorch index). Since `cx`, `cy`, `innerR`, `outerR` are constant after spawn, build the gradient once and mutate only `globalAlpha` per frame. The alpha is applied via `ctx.globalAlpha` before `ctx.fill()`, so a single pre-built gradient (with color-stop alpha=1) scaled by `globalAlpha` works correctly.
acceptance_criteria: CanvasGradient per scorch is allocated once at spawn, not once per frame
Scorch fade-out appearance unchanged

title: syncFire rebuilds a new array + sort on every burning tick
evidence: `syncFire()` (called every FIRING tick from `processFire()` and from `igniteNapalm()`) allocates a fresh `cells: { x: number; life: number }[]` array, iterates the `fire` Map to push into it, then calls `cells.sort(…)`. The napalm fire field can be up to 2×maxSpread columns wide (roughly 60–120 entries). At 60fps, during a napalm burn this is ~60 fresh array allocations + sorts per second. The comment says it avoids a spread+map+sort chain, but the single array + sort is still a per-tick allocation.
impact: Minor GC pressure on each engine tick while napalm burns (can last ~60+ ticks). Not render-critical (engine ticks are fast), but adds to GC pressure on a lower-end device. Low severity because napalm is a specific, time-limited weapon, not the common case.
recommendation: Maintain a pre-allocated `state.fire` array that is updated in-place: instead of rebuilding from scratch, track a `fireDirty` flag. Only rebuild the sorted snapshot when columns are added or removed (ignite/expire), not when only `life` values decrement. Decrement can happen in-place on the existing `state.fire` elements (life-- in place), bypassing the full rebuild on the majority of decay-only ticks.
acceptance_criteria: No array allocation in syncFire on ticks where only `life` values changed (no ignite/expire)
Sorted order is maintained across all ticks
Engine determinism tests pass (scripts/checks/)

title: drawSun creates a new RadialGradient every frame it is called
evidence: `drawSun()` is called from `drawSky()` which is called from `render()` on EVERY frame (even static ones not gated by `isAnimating`). `drawSun()` calls `ctx.createRadialGradient(cx, cy, 0, cx, cy, r)` unconditionally. The sun parameters (cx, cy, r, color stops) are CONSTANTS — they never change during a game. This creates and discards one CanvasGradient per rendered frame.
impact: At 60fps during animation phases (FIRING/RESOLVING), this is 60 throwaway CanvasGradient objects per second. The sky IS redrawn every frame as the base clear pass. Mild but free to fix.
recommendation: Cache the sun gradient in a private field on `Renderer`, alongside `skyGradient`, initialized in `drawSun()` the first time it is needed and reused on subsequent calls. The sun position and colors are invariant (constants from the theme), so invalidation is never needed.
acceptance_criteria: drawSun allocates its CanvasGradient exactly once per Renderer instance lifecycle
Appearance unchanged

title: computeNextSeat clones the entire GameEngine (including 720k-byte terrain bitmap copy) on every turn-ending action
evidence: `computeNextSeat()` calls `this.engine.clone()` (NetworkClient.ts:787). `clone()` in GameEngine.ts deep-copies the entire engine state including `this.terrain.slice()` — a 1200×600 = 720,000-byte Uint8Array copy — plus all tank state, explosion arrays, projectile arrays, and re-creates the wind RNG by fast-forwarding it. This is called for every turn-ending action (fire, use_shield) before submitting it. In a long game with many turns, this is O(match_length) × 720k byte allocations.
impact: Each `computeNextSeat` call allocates ~720kB + overhead. In a 2-player 30-turn game, that's 30 allocations of ~750kB each on the main thread during the time-critical submit path. GC pauses from these short-lived large allocations contribute to jank. The clone is then ticked to completion (up to 10k ticks) before being discarded.
recommendation: The ONLY purpose of the clone is to find the next active player index after a fire/shield resolves. Instead of cloning the full engine, compute the next seat from the local engine's existing alive-tank rotation logic — a simple array scan over `state.tanks` is O(N) and allocation-free. The `computeNextSeat` function only uses `st.activePlayerId` and `st.phase` from the post-tick clone. A lightweight `computeNextSeatIndex(state, action)` helper can derive this without a clone.
acceptance_criteria: computeNextSeat does not clone the GameEngine (no Uint8Array allocation for terrain copy)
nextActiveIndex reported to the referee is identical to the clone-derived value for all action types including round-ending blows
Existing referee tests pass

title: EffectsRenderer.update filters all 4 particle arrays unconditionally even when they are empty
evidence: `update()` iterates all of `this.debris`, `this.smoke`, `this.sparks`, `this.texts` unconditionally with `for..of` updates followed by `.filter()`. The guard `if (this.debris.length)` before the filter (line 195) does prevent a no-op filter, but the four `for..of` update loops still run each frame regardless of whether the arrays have any live entries. During normal PLAYER_TURN with no active effects (the common case, especially on the idle-skip path), all four `for..of` loops are empty iterations but `update()` is still called once per frame by `Renderer.render()`.
impact: Negligible on its own — empty `for..of` is a near-zero cost. However, `Renderer.render()` calls `this.effects.update()` before the `isAnimating` idle-skip check, so update() runs on EVERY state change callback (60fps). This is a missed micro-optimization but true cost is sub-microsecond.
recommendation: Add a length guard at the top of `update()`: `if (!this.debris.length && !this.smoke.length && !this.sparks.length && !this.texts.length) return;` — same pattern as `draw()` already uses (line 203). This makes the no-effect case a single comparison branch.
acceptance_criteria: update() returns early when all particle arrays are empty
draw() already has this guard — add the same to update()

title: submit_action Edge Function fetches the full room row (SELECT *) when only 3 fields are needed
evidence: The `submit_action` function fetches `.select('*')` on the rooms table (line 109). The handler then reads only `room.players`, `room.active_player_index`, and `room.turn` from the result. The rooms table includes `options` (JSONB), `seed` (BIGINT), `winner`, `created_at`, and `rematch_room_id` (migration 002) — none of which are read in this function. `players` is an array of player objects (name, color, ready, lastSeen, ai) that can be non-trivial in size.
impact: Minor over-fetching on a hot path. `submit_action` is called once per turn-ending player action; reducing its payload from full star to 3 columns saves wire bandwidth between Supabase's data API and the Edge Function. On a t3.micro with many concurrent rooms, the saved `options` JSONB and `players` JSONB reduce memory pressure in the Edge Function runtime.
recommendation: Change `.select('*')` to `.select('players, active_player_index, turn, status')` to fetch only the columns the function actually reads. (status is also checked via `.eq('status', 'active')` filter but having it in the result is useful for the null guard.)
acceptance_criteria: submit_action selects only the 4 columns it reads
No behavior change to authorization logic

title: getServiceClient creates a new Supabase client instance on every function invocation
evidence: `getServiceClient()` calls `createClient(supabaseUrl, supabaseServiceKey)` unconditionally on every call. In Deno Deploy, Edge Functions may be long-lived per isolate — `createClient` constructs HTTP client state, connection pools, and GoTrue/Realtime sub-clients. In `enforceRateLimit` (called from `withCors` before the handler), it calls `getServiceClient()` AGAIN, meaning `submit_action` invokes `createClient` twice per request (once in `enforceRateLimit`, once in the handler body at line 104).
impact: Creating a fresh SDK client per request (and twice per `submit_action` call) prevents HTTP keep-alive reuse between calls in the same isolate, wastes construction overhead, and makes the `bump_rate_limit` RPC and the `submit_room_action` RPC use different underlying clients. On a busy server with many turns/second, the double-client construction in submit_action becomes the per-request fixed overhead.
recommendation: Module-level singleton: `const _client = getServiceClient()` once at module load (where env vars are guaranteed present in a deployed Edge Function). Then `getServiceClient()` returns the cached client. In Deno, module state persists for the lifetime of the isolate. Guard with a `try/catch` at module load to convert startup failures to the MissingEnvError.
acceptance_criteria: getServiceClient() returns the same object on repeated calls within a Deno isolate
submit_action makes at most one Supabase client instance per isolate lifetime

title: TerrainRenderer correctly uses terrainVersion dirty-flag — no full hash/scan per frame
evidence: Verified that TerrainRenderer.draw() compares `version !== this.lastVersion` (line 91) before calling `rebuild()`. No FNV hash or Uint8Array scan occurs per frame. The comment in the file explicitly acknowledges that the prior version hashed the full bitmap and that this was replaced with the version-int comparison (P2-8 fix). The 720k-byte offscreen rebuild only runs when `terrainVersion` changes.
impact: None — this is a confirmed non-issue. The dirty-flag design is working as intended.
recommendation: No action needed. The terrain rendering hot path is correct.
acceptance_criteria: No regression: terrainVersion comparison continues to gate the rebuild
